Enlisted as a private at age fourteen ("Spencer Family"). Later deacon of the Hadlyme church.

narrative_blocks.body: Israel enlisted as a private in the Revolutionary War at the age of fourteen. A Hadlyme church deacon for most of his life, he followed his children south to Port Gibson, Mississippi, where his gravestone reads: “The Gospel was his joy and song.”

military_service.notes: Served as a private at age 14 per Spencer family records.
Enlisted as a private at age fourteen ("Spencer Family"). Later deacon of the Hadlyme church.

narrative_blocks.body: Israel enlisted as a private in the Revolutionary War at the age of fourteen. A Hadlyme church deacon for most of his life, he followed his children south to Port Gibson, Mississippi, where his gravestone reads: “The Gospel was his joy and song.”

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty years at sea, then Kansas soil
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel went to sea at twenty and spent upwards of thirty years aboard ships, most of that time as captain of a vessel. Widowed, he came ashore for good in 1875 and took up farming near Wellsville, Kansas.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried Yale learning to the frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from Yale in 1821 just as his family's fortune collapsed, Spencer taught school and read law in frontier Georgia before settling at Port Gibson, Mississippi, in 1828. There he built a leading legal practice in his adopted state.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker and railroad builder of Port Gibson
narrative_blocks.body: Spencer headed the Port Gibson Bank and served for years as president of the Port Gibson and Grand Gulf Railroad, one of Mississippi's earliest lines, which he was chiefly instrumental in building. He also acquired the Almont plantation and large landholdings in Mississippi and Louisiana.

narrative_blocks.header: A Presbyterian elder for forty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: For forty-five years Spencer served as an elder and the chief financial support of the Presbyterian Church in Port Gibson. He gave careful attention to his children's schooling, maintaining a private school under superior teachers.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale-trained Mississippi lawyer, banker, and railroad president

career.location: Hillsboro, Georgia and Port Gibson, Mississippi

narrative_blocks.body: Maria died in 1836, aged about eight.
Horatio died in 1830, in his first year.
Horatio died in 1835, aged about two. His brother Samuel died in the same year.
Samuel died in 1835, aged about one. His brother Horatio died in the same year.
William died in 1841, in his first year. His parents gave the name to a brother born eight years later.

narrative_blocks.header: A pioneering ear surgeon of the Midwest
narrative_blocks.body: The third surviving son, Horatio became a distinguished otologist, practicing in St. Louis and helping to professionalize the young specialty of ear medicine in America. He died while summering at Atlantic City in 1915.
John died in 1847, in his first year.
William died in 1850, aged about one.

notable.notable_blurb: Mississippi U.S. Representative and Confederate veteran

military_service.notes: Enlisted 26 April 1863; served in the Army of Mississippi and Army of Tennessee; captured at Blakely, Alabama, 9 April 1865.

narrative_blocks.header: He followed a Hooker into Congress unrelated
narrative_blocks.body: Elected to the Fifty-fourth Congress in 1894, Spencer took the Mississippi seat just vacated by Charles E. Hooker, a Confederate colonel who, despite the shared surname, has no documented kinship to the Thomas Hooker line from which Spencer descended. A genuine Hooker descendant thus replaced a man merely named Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Captured in the war's final week
narrative_blocks.body: Spencer enlisted as a private in Cowan's Battery of the First Mississippi Light Artillery in 1863 and served through the Army of Mississippi and the Army of Tennessee. He was captured at Blakely, Alabama, on April 9, 1865, in one of the last engagements of the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: From cotton fields to the Capitol
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Spencer returned to Port Gibson and worked in agriculture, real estate, and insurance before entering politics. He served in the Mississippi House from 1892 to 1894 and then a single term in the United States Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of a Yale-trained frontier patriarch
narrative_blocks.body: James was the sixth of ten children of Horatio Nelson Spencer, a Connecticut-born Yale graduate who became a Port Gibson banker, railroad president, and planter. The Spencers carried their Hooker descent from New England into the Mississippi cotton country.

burial.plot_notes: Hadlyme Church Cemetery, Rte 82, East Haddam, Middlesex County, CT. Inscription: "Sacred to the Memory of Mrs. Elizabeth Spencer Consort of Israel Spencer ESQ."

Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
